title: Fourth-party concentration risk — shared critical dependencies across vendors
exclusions: Does NOT rate the fourth-party itself; focuses on concentration score only.
evidence: Monthly 'fourth_party_concentration' writes concentration scores to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: DORA Art.28 requires institutions to assess concentration risk in ICT outsourcing; the UC provides the quantitative foundation.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that DORA Art.28 (ICT third-party risk) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.44.3: Fourth-party concentration risk — shared critical dependencies across vendors.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.44.3' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When a fourth-party is a critical dependency for ≥3 high-criticality vendors, the UC fires with the fourth-party id and the concentration score (>0.6).
controlTest.negativeScenario: Fourth-parties with lower concentration OR lower upstream criticality do NOT fire.

spl:
| inputlookup vendor_dependency_graph.csv
| where vendor_criticality IN ("critical","high")
| stats dc(vendor) as dependent_count, values(vendor) as vendors by fourth_party
| eval concentration_score=min(dependent_count/3,1)
| where concentration_score>=0.6
| table fourth_party dependent_count vendors concentration_score
description: Computes a monthly concentration score for each fourth-party dependency across the vendor graph. Surfaces candidates for additional due-diligence or contractual controls.
value: Addresses the DORA supervisory-priorities theme that regulators ask 'who do your vendors depend on?'.
implementation: (1) Maintain vendor_dependency_graph.csv enriched monthly; (2) run monthly; (3) surface to risk committee.
visualization: Network graph of concentration, bar chart of fourth-parties by score, single-value 'fourth-parties above concentration threshold'.
knownFalsePositives: Depth-1 dependencies where the upstream vendor has alternate providers should be discounted (substitutability>0).
grandmaExplanation: Addresses the DORA supervisory-priorities theme that regulators ask 'who do your vendors depend on?' — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: See subcategory preamble..
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain vendor_dependency_graph.csv enriched monthly; (2) run monthly; (3) surface to risk committee.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| inputlookup vendor_dependency_graph.csv
| where vendor_criticality IN ("critical","high")
| stats dc(vendor) as dependent_count, values(vendor) as vendors by fourth_party
| eval concentration_score=min(dependent_count/3,1)
| where concentration_score>=0.6
| table fourth_party dependent_count vendors concentration_score
```

Understanding this SPL

**Fourth-party concentration risk — shared critical dependencies across vendors** — Addresses the DORA supervisory-priorities theme that regulators ask 'who do your vendors depend on?'.

Documented **Data sources**: See subcategory preamble. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Loads rows via `inputlookup` (KV store or CSV lookup) for enrichment or reporting.
• Filters the current rows with `where vendor_criticality IN ("critical","high")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by fourth_party** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **concentration_score** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where concentration_score>=0.6` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Fourth-party concentration risk — shared critical dependencies across vendors**): table fourth_party dependent_count vendors concentration_score


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Network graph of concentration, bar chart of fourth-parties by score, single-value 'fourth-parties above concentration threshold'.
